items.text: How to get an Opal card

title: How to Get an Opal Card
introduction: This page explains the different Opal cards and how to get the card that is right for you.
openingParagraphs: You use an Opal card to pay for public transport in New South Wales.
There are different cards for different people.
This guide explains the choices and the steps you may need to follow.

sections.title: What an Opal card does
introductionParagraphs: An Opal card helps you pay for trips on public transport.

blocks.title: Pay for public transport

paragraphs.text: An Opal card stores money for your trips.
You can use it on buses, trains, light rail and ferries.

blocks.imgAlt: An Opal card surrounded by a bus, train, light rail vehicle and ferry

blocks.title: Tap on and tap off

paragraphs.text: Tap your card on the reader when you start your trip.
Tap it again when you finish.
The reader works out the correct fare.

blocks.imgAlt: A hand tapping a blue card on a public transport card reader

sections.title: Choose the right card
introductionParagraphs: Check which card matches your age and circumstances.

blocks.title: Adult Opal card or contactless payment

paragraphs.text: Most adults can use an adult Opal card. You can also tap an accepted bank card or phone instead.
Contactless payment means you pay by tapping without inserting or swiping a card.

blocks.imgAlt: A wallet with a travel card and a phone showing contactless payment

blocks.title: Child and youth Opal card

paragraphs.text: Children aged 4 to 15 can use a child and youth Opal card.
This card charges a lower fare than an adult card.

blocks.imgAlt: A young person with a backpack tapping a card beside a bus

blocks.title: School Opal card

paragraphs.text: Some school and TAFE students can get free travel between home and their place of study.
The card is for approved trips on school days during the school term.

blocks.imgAlt: A school backpack, travel card, school building, bus and calendar

blocks.title: Concession Opal card

paragraphs.text: Some students, apprentices, trainees and other eligible people can use a concession Opal card.
It charges a lower fare.
You must meet the rules before you apply.

blocks.imgAlt: A student identification card, travel card, books and graduation cap

blocks.title: Gold Opal card

paragraphs.text: Eligible seniors and pensioners can use a Gold Opal card.
The card limits how much they pay for public transport each day.
Other eligible people may also be able to apply.

blocks.imgAlt: An older person holding a gold travel card beside identification and a train

blocks.title: Free travel card

paragraphs.text: Some people who are blind or have low vision can receive free travel.
Some people who meet disability or Australian Defence Force rules may also receive free travel.
You must show that you meet the rules.

blocks.imgAlt: A hand tapping a travel card near a white mobility cane

sections.title: Check if you can pay a lower fare
introductionParagraphs: You may need to provide information before you can get a concession card.

blocks.title: Further education students

paragraphs.text: If you study at TAFE, university or college, check whether your institution supports concession travel.
You may need to allow the institution to share your enrolment details with Transport for NSW.

blocks.imgAlt: A student at a desk with identification and travel cards beside an open book

blocks.title: Apprentices and trainees

paragraphs.text: Apprentices and trainees may qualify for concession travel.
You may need the identification number from your training contract when you apply.

blocks.imgAlt: Work boots, a hard hat, wrench, training contract and travel card

sections.title: Get your card
introductionParagraphs: The way you get a card depends on the type of card you need.

blocks.title: Buy an adult or child card in a store

paragraphs.text: You can get adult and child cards from participating retailers and some customer service locations.
Ask the retailer which cards are available.

blocks.imgAlt: Hands exchanging a travel card and receipt at a shop counter

blocks.title: Order a card online

paragraphs.text: You can order some Opal cards online.
Enter your delivery address carefully.
Your card will be sent to you.

blocks.imgAlt: A tablet with a delivery form and an envelope containing a travel card

blocks.title: Apply for a school card

paragraphs.text: Apply for school travel using the school student travel service.
Your school may need to confirm your enrolment.

blocks.imgAlt: A parent and child completing a school travel application on a laptop

blocks.title: Get an entitlement card first

paragraphs.text: Get a Transport Concession Entitlement Card before applying for a concession Opal card.
The entitlement card proves that you meet the concession rules.

blocks.imgAlt: A hand holding an entitlement card with an arrow pointing to a travel card

blocks.title: Apply for a Gold or free travel card

paragraphs.text: Use the application option that matches your eligibility.
You may need a Seniors Card, Pensioner Concession Card, travel pass or other evidence. Check which evidence applies to you before you start.

blocks.imgAlt: A phone showing gold and free travel card choices beside identification

sections.title: Before you travel
introductionParagraphs: Check what you need to carry and allow enough time for your card to arrive.

blocks.title: Carry both cards

paragraphs.text: Carry your entitlement card and concession Opal card together.
A transport officer may ask to see both cards.

blocks.imgAlt: An open wallet holding an entitlement card and a concession travel card

blocks.title: Apply before your visit

paragraphs.text: Older visitors to New South Wales should apply for a Gold Opal card before their trip.
Allow at least 3 weeks for the application and delivery.

blocks.imgAlt: A suitcase, gold travel card and calendar showing time before a trip

sections.title: Get help
introductionParagraphs: Support is available if you need help to understand the information or choose a card.

blocks.title: Ask for language or communication support

paragraphs.text: Ask for information in another language or an accessible format if you need it.
You can use the Translating and Interpreting Service or ask someone you trust for support.

blocks.imgAlt: Hands using a transport information app with language, audio and interpreter support

blocks.title: Contact Transport for NSW

paragraphs.text: Contact Transport for NSW if you are not sure which card to get.
Call 13 77 88 or use the Transport for NSW contact service.

blocks.imgAlt: A phone showing a customer service person beside bus, train and telephone symbols

about.aboutDescription: This page uses Easy Read. Easy Read uses simple words, clear structure and meaningful pictures to make information easier to understand.
